data: Dedication to the Emperor Lucius Aurelius Verus
Iscrizioni latine lapidarie del Museo di Palermo
Livia Bivona, Iscrizioni latine lapidarie del Museo di Palermo, Sikelia 5 (Palermo: Flaccovio, 1970)
A large marble plaque, broken into multiple fragments, and of which the lower left corner is missing. The fragments have been joined together in a plaster setting with the lacunae filed in.
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana; photo J. Prag 2017-07-21
Partially reopened following extensive restoration work. Comprehensive collection of archaeological material, predominantly from the West of the island, with an important Etruscan collection.
Centre of Palermo, Piazza Olivella 24, 90100 - Palermo

Facsimile Images

Apparatus criticus

  • Text of IMusPalermo

English translation

Alfredo Tosques

To Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Antoninus, grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holder of tribunician power, consul for the second time, father of the fatherland, the Colonia Augusta of the Tyndaritani, by decree of the decurions, under the supervision of Marcus Valerius Vitalis, curator of the public affairs.

Italian translation

Alfredo Tosques

All’imperatore Cesare, figlio del divo Antonino, nipote del divo Adriano, pronipote del divo Traiano Partico, trisnipote del divo Nerva, Lucio Aurelio Vero Augusto, pontefice massimo, investito della potestà tribunizia, console per la seconda volta, padre della patria, la Colonia Augusta dei Tindaritani, per decreto dei decurioni, sotto la supervisione di Marco Valerio Vitale, curatore degli affari pubblici.

commentary

This inscription together with a second, ISic000065, both erected by Marcus Valerius Vitalis, honours the emperors Lucius Verus and Marcus Aurelius, who shared imperial power from 161 to 169 CE. They also attest to the presence of a curator rei publicae, an imperial official appointed by Rome to oversee local administration. Cities generally enjoyed a high degree of autonomy, and the reasons for this external supervision are unclear, though they may have been linked to financial difficulties.

Text of IMusPalermo

To Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Antoninus, grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holder of tribunician power, consul for the second time, father of the fatherland, the Colonia Augusta of the Tyndaritani, by decree of the decurions, under the supervision of Marcus Valerius Vitalis, curator of the public affairs.

All’imperatore Cesare, figlio del divo Antonino, nipote del divo Adriano, pronipote del divo Traiano Partico, trisnipote del divo Nerva, Lucio Aurelio Vero Augusto, pontefice massimo, investito della potestà tribunizia, console per la seconda volta, padre della patria, la Colonia Augusta dei Tindaritani, per decreto dei decurioni, sotto la supervisione di Marco Valerio Vitale, curatore degli affari pubblici.

This inscription together with a second, ISic000065, both erected by Marcus Valerius Vitalis, honours the emperors Lucius Verus and Marcus Aurelius, who shared imperial power from 161 to 169 CE. They also attest to the presence of a curator rei publicae, an imperial official appointed by Rome to oversee local administration. Cities generally enjoyed a high degree of autonomy, and the reasons for this external supervision are unclear, though they may have been linked to financial difficulties.
